_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
Transport Canada does not impose a standalone EPR-style producer registration on freight forwarders. However, the following registration and reporting obligations are confirmed or proposed across the regulatory publications covered by this item:

**Foreign Air Operator Certificate (FAOC) — TP 11524:** Foreign air operators (not the workspace itself, but its carrier partners) must hold a Minister-issued FAOC before conducting air transport services, code-share operations, or FTA specialty services to/from Canada. The certification process follows a five-phase model: pre-application, formal application, document evaluation, demonstration and inspection, and certification. Applications must be submitted to Transport Canada's Foreign Operations Division (AAROF) with jurisdiction-specific document packages (Annexes A–E of TP 11524). For non-U.S. applicants, Annex A applies; for U.S. applicants, Annex B applies; code-share arrangements follow Annex C; CUSMA specialty services follow Annex D; TPP specialty services follow Annex E. All FAA Operations Specifications presented must bear both operator and FAA signatures (digital or otherwise). The workspace must collect and maintain evidence of valid FAOC status for every air carrier it books on Canadian trans-border lanes.

**Dangerous Goods — TDG Regulations:** Consignors tendering dangerous goods for air transport in Canada are not required to hold a federal licence per se but must maintain shipping documents, classification records, and training certificates. An emergency response assistance plan (ERAP) may be required for certain high-hazard commodities. Equivalency certificates (currently still required in some cases, targeted for elimination by the pending harmonisation amendments) must be obtained from Transport Canada where goods do not fully conform to the prescribed standards. The workspace should collect: UN number, proper shipping name, hazard class, packing group, quantity, and emergency contact (CANUTEC: 613-996-6666) for each DG shipment on record.

**Aviation Security Regulations (proposed amendment):** Once finalised, air carriers holding Part VII certificates will be required to obtain Ministerial approval for a written security program aligned with ICAO Annex 17. The workspace should collect from its carrier partners confirmation of security program status once the rule comes into force (estimated 3 years after Canada Gazette Part II publication). No registration obligation falls directly on the freight forwarder.

**Pilot Readback / AC 602-008:** A new Advisory Circular on pilot readback requirements was published in late 2025; the associated regulatory requirement becomes applicable approximately November 2026. No reporting obligation for freight forwarders, but awareness is needed for operational coordination with flight operations contacts.

*Source: Foreign Air Operator Certification Manual TP 11524 (2026 edition), Transport Canada. https://tc.canada.ca/en/aviation/publications/foreign-air-operator-certification-manual-tp-11524. Canada Gazette Part I, 25 April 2026, Regulations Amending the Canadian Aviation Security Regulations, 2012. https://gazette.gc.ca/rp-pr/p1/2026/2026-04-25/html/reg2-eng.html. Transport Canada Red Tape Progress Report. https://tc.canada.ca/en/corporate-services/acts-regulations/reducing-red-tape-transportation-sector/transport-canada-progress-report-red-tape-review.*
